feedback.text: Hello, I'd like to express my interest in extending this initiative to include the guidelines outlined by Stop Destroying Videogames. In addition, I the consumer, want all of this: - Sold video games to be protected as a purchased item that we own, and to be treated as such, ensuring full autonomy and replayability. Should online services shut down, the offline modes remain playable. A final patch ensuring offline play mode compatibility should be made by video game developers prior to closing servers down. - Once a video game has been bought, ownership directly goes to the buyer. Intellectual property still reminds by the game developers, however, the buyer can now use the game however they like. A person should not be punished for using their property as they want. This includes modding or editing game files. This includes modifying gaming consoles. Should a person be hacking and distrupting online multiplayer games, their console cannot be made unusable (I.e. the current practice with Nintendo Switch 2), rather, their user shall be blacklisted from online multiplayer play. They can still play and purchase offline games, or play other online games. - Video games to remain freely available and able to be paid for by any type of card/cash, in person or online. - ID checks to apply only in person. It is nonsense to apply ID checks online as persons with cards are adults, or have parental guidance. Perhaps make it so student bank cards cannot purchase 18+ games but otherwise this should remain unlimited. - Video game developers must include information of if their games have online and/or offline modes prior to purchase, either on a store page or on physical cases. - Video games modes that do not require internet can be played without internet. No forced online play. Saves can be made locally if a person does not wish to use the cloud save. - Purchasing of video game money with real life currency requires a CAPTCHA and select/type exactly how much they want to spend, and confirm twice. Forced fixed in game currency purchases cannot be allowed. - All bugs in video games must be addressed (either with a comment or fix) within 6-12 months of first being reported to the game development team. As we bought an incomplete and faulty product, this must be fixed within reasonable time or we deserve a refund. - When purchasing a game online, all games must give the purchaser a receipt or a key (in email or to their account) which would allow them to repurchase the game on another digital account (which would result in the first digital copy being deleted) should they lose access to the digital copy they have. Consumers must have the option to purchase a physical copy too, either at full or reduced price (if they already have a digital copy). This must be easy for the consumer to access, preferably off the developer's store page or in store. Should game developers not be able to afford physical copies, they should increase the price of the game to account for the cost. - Consumer data cannot be sold without consent. A consumer may opt-in for their digital data to be sold/used by the developer. This cannot be an opt-out policy. - Any use of artificial intelligence (AI) during game development must be made clear to the consumer. It must be clear how AI was used. Failure to report can result in fines or removal from online store pages and in store shelf space. - Video games with potential harmful content (violence/bodily harm/physiological terror) MUST include a screen upon each start up detailing the harmful content in plain text and this is unskippable. A sound saying "Dear parent or guardian" should play as well to catch the attention of parents/guardians. - Video games can only have advertisements in a 'store' area of them game with no forced entry into the store page. No in game advertisements, including pop-ups. Thank you for your time and consideration. The digital space is important to me and I hope to see it continue to thrive!
